introduction: A neglected tropical disease. In Africa, snake bite often occurs among farmers who walk unshod, and occasionally occur around homes when snakes are accidentally stepped upon.

features: Depends on the type of snake, location of bite and promptness of intervention
Local effects include Pain, swelling, bruising, tender enlargement of regional lymph nodes
Local and systemic bleeding, impairment of organ function, reduction of cardiac output, inhibition of peripheral nerve impulses
Multisystem effects: Rhabdomyolysis, Haemolysis, Blood vessel damage
Snake bite wounds may become secondarily infected with: Clostridium tetani, causing tetanus, Clostridium welchii, causing gas gangrene

investigations: Test for haemostasis (20 Minute Whole Blood Clotting Test)

non_drug: Move the victim to safety from area where they may have been bitten
Reassure the patient (only 50% of the bites by venomous snakes cause envenoming)
Remove constricting clothing, rings, shoes, bracelets, bands from the bitten limb
Immobilize the whole patient, especially the bitten limb, using a splint or sling
Transport the patient to the hospital as quickly and passively as possible
Avoid harmful practices: never attempt to suck out or aspirate the poison, do not make incisions, never attempt to catch the snake, a tight arterial tourniquet should never be used)
Assess and observe the patient for at least 24 hours
Identification of the snake would help in the choice of anti-venom (where specific anti-venoms are available)
Wound debridement and fasciotomy for compartment syndrome may become necessary
drug: Intravenous fluid administration to maintain circulation: use colloids or crystalloids as clinically appropriate
Anti-venom: Usually polyvalent anti-venoms are used, but where specific species are identified, a monospecific anti-venom may be used
No dose difference between adult and child in terms of dosage. Contents of the anti-venom vial diluted in sodium chloride 0.9% intravenous infusion, and infused intravenously over 30 minutes
adverse_reactions_and_cautions: Early reactions (3 - 60 minutes): Cough, tachycardia, itching, urticarial, fever, nausea, vomiting, headache
Treatment of anaphylaxis with antihistamines (H blockers), epinephrine (adrenaline) and corticosteroids
Pyrogenic reactions; due to pyrogen contamination of anti-venom during manufacture - begins 1-2 hours after treatment and characterized by fever and chills
Tepid sponging and administration of paracetamol are useful

Late reactions: Occurs 5 - 24 days (average 7 days) following anti-venom - Characterized by itching, urticaria, fever, arthralgia, periarticular swellings, proteinuria and sometimes neurological symptoms
Antihistamines are used for milder attacks, but in severe cases, including those with neurological symptoms, a short course of prednisolone should be used
supportive_measures: Cardiopulmonary resuscitation may be necessary: clearing of the airway, oxygen administration by face mask or nasal cannula, intravenous access to allow treatment of shock with intravenous fluids
